localBiteReport: Reports from the past week out of the Santa Rosa Beach/Destin area of Choctawhatchee Bay show speckled trout as the standout inshore species, with multiple guides noting a strong and widespread trout bite across the bay despite some windy and choppy conditions making spot selection tricky. Redfish are also actively showing up, including a notable bull red over 40 inches caught in the area within the last six days. One Gulf Breeze-area guide noted the inshore bite had been tough overall but was improving daily heading into this week. Water temps are sitting in the low 80s, which is typical for July and generally keeps fish active but scattered. Note: the most detailed written reports available are from captainexperiences.com and are 6–14 days old; no fresh charter blog reports dated this specific week (July 7–10) were found.

aiSummary: Choctawhatchee Bay is fishing well heading into mid-July, with guides reporting a strong and widespread speckled trout bite across the bay and redfish showing up consistently — including a bull red over 40 inches caught in the area recently. Today starts with mostly sunny skies and light westerly winds of 5–10 mph before a 20% chance of afternoon storms rolls in, so plan to be on the water early and keep an eye on the sky after midday. The new moon's spring tide effect will push strong tidal movement through cuts and creek mouths, which should concentrate bait and trigger aggressive feeding across most locations.

bridge.aiNote: The 331 Bridge spans the narrow neck of Choctawhatchee Bay, creating a current funnel where bait gets pushed through on both tides. Pilings hold speckled trout, redfish, sheepshead, and black drum year-round, with the lee-side pilings serving as the prime ambush zone on incoming tides. It's also the most weather-exposed spot on this list — open water and chop can make it unfishable when wind picks up.
bridge.todaysCall: The incoming tide running through the bridge pilings this morning sets up a textbook scenario — hit the lee-side pilings early while the current is moving and the sky is still clear. Light westerly winds at 5–10 mph will put some ripple on the water but shouldn't shut things down if you work the sheltered side of the structure. The new moon spring tide will push bait through the funnel with extra authority, so fish the current seam with live shrimp or a suspending lure tight to the pilings. With storms possible before 4 PM, plan to wrap up here by early afternoon — this is the first spot to turn dangerous when weather builds, and there's no shelter to duck into.

alaqua.aiNote: Alaqua Bayou offers protected water with a strong year-round redfish population, plus trout and flounder at the mouth where oyster bars are the prime target. Tree cover keeps interior water a few degrees cooler than the open bay and provides real shelter from wind and building weather. The upper creek transitions toward brackish and can hold bass alongside smaller reds.
alaqua.todaysCall: The tree-lined interior makes Alaqua a smart call today — you'll get natural shade against the heat index pushing toward 100°F and a buffer from the westerly wind. Focus the early morning hours on the oyster bars at the creek mouth while the tide is still falling from the 7:04 AM high, as bait will be pushed off the flats and concentrating on those edges. Slightly stained water today favors redfish, who will be hunting by feel and scent along the oyster structure — a slow-rolled gold spoon or cut mullet on bottom are reliable choices. If storms fire up before 4 PM, Alaqua's tree cover gives you a reasonable place to wait it out compared to more exposed spots.

basin.aiNote: Basin Bayou sees less fishing pressure than Alaqua and LaGrange, which means the fish here tend to be less educated and often more willing to eat. Interior grass flats are classic sight-fishing territory for reds in under two feet of water, and pompano occasionally show at the mouth on moving tides in late spring and early summer. It's less protected than LaGrange when weather builds, so it's worth keeping that in mind if conditions deteriorate.
basin.todaysCall: With the tide dropping from the morning high through the afternoon, reds will be pushed off the shallow interior grass flats and condensing into the deeper cuts and creek edges — find those transition zones and you'll find fish. The new moon spring tide means the drop will be pronounced and relatively fast, so the window for skinny-water sight-fishing is short; get in there early, ideally within the first hour or two after sunrise. Slightly stained water reduces your sight-fishing advantage a bit, so a weedless gold spoon or popping cork with live shrimp will be more reliable than relying on your eyes alone. Keep the storm window in mind — Basin offers less weather protection than LaGrange, so if clouds build aggressively before 4 PM, be ready to make a move.

lagrange.aiNote: LaGrange Bayou is the top trout producer among the bayous in this area, with legendary oyster bars at the mouth and interior grass flats that hold redfish year-round. Deep holes inside the bayou hold black drum and sheepshead, giving you multiple species to target across different structure types. It's also the most protected water on this side of the bay, making it the default choice when weather builds or wind picks up.
lagrange.todaysCall: LaGrange is the strongest all-around play today — the bay-wide trout bite has been exceptional, and this bayou's oyster bar mouth is exactly the kind of structure where that bite concentrates. Work the mouth bars hard during the first couple hours after the 7:04 AM high tide as current moves bait over the oysters; topwater plugs or a popping cork can be deadly in low-light conditions early. The new moon spring tide will amplify tidal flow through the bayou mouth, so bait stacking will be more pronounced than on a neap tide — fish aggressively in the current seam. If afternoon storms fire up, LaGrange is your best shelter option on this side of the bay, so you can fish longer here with confidence than at most other spots on this list.

fourmile.aiNote: Four Mile Creek is a short tidal creek running between Shipyard Marina and the bay, making it both convenient and well-protected. Tidal flow pushes baitfish up the creek on incoming tides, concentrating redfish and trout in the bends and along grass edges. The creek mouth is a prime flounder zone, and the upper section transitions toward brackish water where bass and small reds mix.
fourmile.todaysCall: As the bay's shortest and most protected fishery, Four Mile Creek is an ideal option if morning reports suggest weather is developing faster than forecast. This morning's outgoing tide from the 7:04 AM high will push baitfish back toward the creek mouth — set up at the mouth or just inside the first bend and let the tide do the work, presenting a jig or live shrimp in the current. The spring tide effect from the new moon will make this tidal flush more significant than usual, so bait concentration at the mouth should be solid. Flounder season is always worth a shot at the mouth on these falling tides — a slow-dragged paddletail on bottom is the move.

hogtown.aiNote: Hogtown Bayou is a signature Santa Rosa Beach guide spot and one of the most consistently productive trout flats on Choctawhatchee Bay. Summer mornings are the peak window, with speckled trout stacking on grass in two to four feet of water — this flat is most productive in the early hours before boat traffic and heat build. Spanish mackerel show at the mouth on moving tides, and the bayou sees regular pressure but produces reliably despite it.
hogtown.todaysCall: The bay-wide trout bite has been genuinely strong, and Hogtown's grass flats are exactly where that bite lives — but the early morning window is everything here in July. Be on the flat by first light, well before the heat index climbs toward 100°F and before other boats push in, and work topwater plugs or a popping cork across the two-to-four-foot grass zone. The new moon spring tide will give you a stronger tidal pulse than usual, which tends to activate trout on the flat edges — pay attention to current breaks and weedline transitions. Factor in the run time from Shipyard Marina before you leave the dock, and plan to be heading back or parked in shelter before any afternoon storm activity builds; there's no quick escape from this spot if weather moves in fast.

rocky.aiNote: Rocky Bayou sits inside Fred Gannon Rocky Bayou State Park near Niceville, offering a protected, scenic, and significantly less pressured fishery than the bayous closer to the 331 Bridge. Freshwater influence from Rocky Creek makes flounder fishing excellent, and the glass-calm state park coves are ideal for sight-fishing redfish. Mangrove snapper hold near dock pilings, and the longer run time from Shipyard Marina is a real factor to build into your day.
rocky.todaysCall: Rocky Bayou's state park setting and light fishing pressure make it a genuine sleeper pick — the redfish and flounder here simply don't see the lure selection and boat traffic that fish closer to the bridge endure. The calm, protected coves inside the park are ideal for sight-fishing reds even in slightly stained water; work the shoreline edges and dock pilings with a weedless soft plastic or live shrimp. New moon spring tides will push a solid tidal exchange through the bayou, which should stack baitfish near the park docks and creek mouth — prime mangrove snapper territory worth a few casts on light tackle. Account for the extended run from Shipyard Marina in your planning, and leave yourself extra buffer before the pre-4 PM storm window to avoid being caught out on open water during the return trip.
